title: Shared Libraries
text: Shared Libraries A shared library is a library of compiled 3GL code that is built in such a way that it can be loaded at run time, the code (though not the data) being shared by different applications. This is in contrast with static linking of relocatable code in the executable, where each process had its own copy of the code. Shared libraries are used on all platforms, but they are called differently on different platforms. Table 1. Names for Shared Libraries per Platform Platform Name Windows DLL (Dynamic Link Library) Unix / Linux Shared library or shared executable iSeries (AS/400) Service program We use the neutral term shared library, except when discussing platform-specific issues, in which case we will use the local terminology. The use of shared libraries has the following impact: You make a shared library and its entry points known to Uniface by adding an entry to the assignment file. On all platforms, this is now done in the assignment file using a new assignment section called [USER_3GL] . For more information, see [USER_3GL] . Shared libraries will work only if the environment is set correctly, and if the shared libraries are compatible with those of the software Uniface communicates with (for example, the Oracle shared libraries). The error messages, and the way to set the environment, are platform-specific. For more information, see Loading Shared Libraries . Any 3GL code that is called from Uniface must reside in a shared library. Most third-party code will be available in shared library format, but you might encounter older legacy software that is only available in relocatable archives. It will not be possible to call such code from Uniface directly, although it might be possible to wrap a shared library around it so it can be called indirectly. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
